summary: Controls whether Windows Program Compatibility Assistant (PCA) detects compatibility issues for applications and drivers. Value name: DisablePcaUI under HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\AppCompat. Policy family: pca.admx (DisablePcaUIPolicy). Supported on Windows 8+. enabledValue=1 means detection stays active and disabledValue=0 turns detection off.

setting.name: Disable PCA Compatibility Diagnostics Detection
setting.casual_explanation: Windows PCA watches for compatibility issues when you run applications and drivers, and can show warnings or suggest fixes. This policy controls whether that detection runs. Disabling it stops PCA from actively monitoring for compatibility problems - it does not disable PCA itself (that is controlled separately by DisablePCA).
setting.why_people_change_it: People disable PCA diagnostics detection to reduce background compatibility monitoring on systems running only current, known-compatible software. They leave it enabled when they run older applications that may trigger compatibility warnings.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Disabling detection means PCA will not warn you about compatibility issues even if they occur. On a clean Win11 system with only current software, this has no practical effect. On a system with legacy software, disabling detection may silently swallow compatibility warnings.
setting.professional_notes: Value name DisablePcaUI is misleading - the name suggests 'disable PCA UI', but the policy actually controls compatibility issue detection. pca.admx has explicit enabledValue=1 (detection ON) and disabledValue=0 (detection OFF). To disable diagnostics, write 0, not 1. Policy dependency: if DisablePCA=1 is active (Turn off Program Compatibility Assistant), this policy has no effect since PCA is fully disabled. This policy was previously incorrectly used in the app with value=1, intending to disable PCA - that was a double error: wrong key and wrong value. Corrected in PrivacyTweakProvider.cs on 2026-03-13 (switched to DisablePCA=1). supportedOn: Windows 8 or later.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: PCA detects compatibility issues for applications and drivers. Default Windows behavior.

allowed_values.label: Disable PCA diagnostics detection
allowed_values.meaning: disabledValue in pca.admx. PCA does not detect compatibility issues. Detection and related diagnostics are suppressed. Note: has no effect if DisablePCA=1 is active.

allowed_values.label: PCA diagnostics detection active (policy explicit)
allowed_values.meaning: enabledValue in pca.admx. PCA detects compatibility issues. Equivalent to missing/not configured.

targets.notes: WARNING: value name 'DisablePcaUI' is semantically inverted relative to its behavior. To disable detection, write 0 (disabledValue), not 1. Writing 1 (enabledValue) keeps detection active. This is consistent with pca.admx but counterintuitive from the value name alone. Do not confuse with DisablePCA (separate key, same path) which actually disables PCA entirely.

app_current_implementation.notes: The app now writes DisablePcaUI = 0 on the documented policy path. That matches the disabledValue in pca.admx and keeps this diagnostics-only policy separate from the full DisablePCA switch.

states.rationale: Key absent. PCA detects compatibility issues normally.

recommended_profiles.label: Disable PCA compatibility diagnostics detection
intended_for: Windows systems running only current, known-compatible software
Users who want to reduce background compatibility monitoring
avoid_for: Systems running legacy applications that may trigger compatibility warnings
Enterprise environments where PCA warnings are used for compatibility reporting

states.rationale: disabledValue confirmed in pca.admx. PCA stops detecting compatibility issues. Write 0 to disable detection - writing 1 would keep detection active.

recommended_profiles.good_when: Running a clean Win11 system with only current software. Compatibility detection has no practical effect in this scenario.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: Running older or unknown software where compatibility warnings may be meaningful.
tradeoffs: PCA will not warn about compatibility issues even if they occur.
No known BSOD risk - this is isolated to DisablePcaUI=0, not DisablePCA.
Reversible: remove key or set to 1 and run gpupdate /force.
Has no effect if DisablePCA=1 is active - PCA is already fully disabled in that case.
recommended_profiles.notes: apply_allowed=true. The proof chain is complete: pca.admx explicit enabledValue=1 and disabledValue=0, pca.adml exact behavior text, and the Microsoft Learn ADMX_pca CSP page. Risk is low because this only disables diagnostics detection and does not disable PCA entirely. Procmon capture is optional here because the explicit ADMX numeric mapping is already sufficient for this class of record.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows managed default
intended_for: All users who want normal Windows compatibility monitoring

states.rationale: Leave policy unset. PCA detects compatibility issues normally.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want PCA to actively monitor for compatibility issues.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want to reduce background compatibility monitoring.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft pca.admx - DisablePcaUIPolicy
evidence.summary: DisablePcaUIPolicy block: key=Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\AppCompat, valueName=DisablePcaUI, enabledValue=1 (detection active), disabledValue=0 (detection disabled). supportedOn Windows 8 or later. Explicit numeric direction - no context inference required.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft pca.adml - DisablePcaUIPolicy help text
evidence.summary: Policy display name: 'Detect compatibility issues for applications and drivers'. Enabled: PCA detects issues. Disabled: PCA does not detect issues. Not configured: PCA detects issues. Additional note: if 'Turn off Program Compatibility Assistant' is enabled, this policy has no effect.

evidence.title: Policy CSP - ADMX_pca
evidence.summary: Microsoft Learn CSP page confirming DisablePcaUIPolicy exists and maps to DisablePcaUI under Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\AppCompat. Confirms device scope.

evidence.title: nohuto win-config mirror - DisablePcaUI policy
evidence.summary: pca.admx shows DisablePcaUIPolicy under HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\AppCompat. The mirrored ExplainText says enabling the policy configures PCA to detect application and driver compatibility failures, while disabling it stops detection. EnabledValue=1 and DisabledValue=0 are explicit.

decision.why: The proof chain is complete: pca.admx provides explicit enabledValue and disabledValue mappings, pca.adml provides exact behavior text, and the Microsoft Learn CSP page confirms the policy surface. Numeric direction is explicit and does not rely on inference. Risk is low because this disables diagnostics detection rather than disabling PCA entirely. recommended_for_general_users remains false because the practical benefit is small and the setting needs context to use responsibly.

open_questions: Procmon capture could confirm DisablePcaUI=0 as a runtime write, but that is optional because the explicit ADMX mapping is already sufficient for this record class.
DisablePCA still overrides this child policy when both are set.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: pca.admx: `<policy name="DisablePcaUIPolicy" ... key="Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\AppCompat" valueName="DisablePcaUI">` with `<enabledValue><decimal value="1" /></enabledValue>` and `<disabledValue><decimal value="0" /></disabledValue>` and `supportedOn ref="windows:SUPPORTED_Windows8"`; pca.adml: `If you enable this policy setting, the PCA is configured to detect failures...` and `If you disable this policy setting, the PCA does not detect compatibility issues for applications and drivers.`
validation_proof.notes: Local official pca.admx and pca.adml files document the exact DisablePcaUI policy surface and semantics. The Microsoft Learn ADMX_pca CSP page confirms the same device-scoped policy mapping. Added nohuto mirror corroboration via nohuto-pca-diagnostics-admx.
